content.text: Model Card Templates for Claude Deployments

A model card is a structured document that describes an AI system — its intended use, known limitations, evaluation results, and governance metadata. Originally proposed by Mitchell et al. (2019) for ML models, model cards have become an expected artifact for enterprise AI deployments: the EU AI Act's technical documentation requirements (Annex IV), NIST AI RMF's transparency profile, and enterprise procurement questionnaires all ask questions that a well-maintained model card answers.

For Claude deployments, the model card operates at the application layer — it describes the specific Claude integration, not the base Claude model (Anthropic publishes its own model cards for the base model). This distinction matters because the enterprise is responsible for how Claude is configured, constrained, and monitored in their system.

attrs.alt: Model Card Anatomy for a Claude Deployment

content.text: Model Card vs. System Card — Terminology
The terms are sometimes used interchangeably, but there is a practical distinction:
The underlying Claude model: training approach, benchmark results, known biases, intended use cases
A specific Claude integration: the system prompt, data inputs, output use, human oversight, evaluation against the deployment's task
The training or evaluation data used to test the deployment

Anthropic publishes model cards for Claude models on its website. Enterprises building on Claude should link to Anthropic's model card in their own system card rather than re-documenting the base model characteristics they did not control.
The EU AI Act Annex IV defines the technical documentation required for high-risk AI systems. The sections below map the Annex IV requirements to the model card structure:
General description of the AI system: intended purpose, version, hardware/software
Section 1: Model Identity + Section 2: Intended Use
Design specifications: general logic, design choices, assumptions
Section 2: Intended Use + system prompt summary
Description of the system's elements and their development process
Section 1: links to Anthropic's model card; Section 7: integration architecture
Description of the human oversight measures
Pre-determined changes and their expected impact
Section 7: Maintenance & Versioning — planned upgrade schedule
Monitoring, functioning, and control of the AI system
Description of the validation and testing procedures
Section 5: Evaluation Results — methodology and thresholds
Description of any changes made throughout the lifecycle

# Section 1: Model Identity
model_id: "CS-SUPPORT-001"
display_name: "Customer Support Draft Generator"
base_model:
  provider: "Anthropic"
  model_id: "claude-opus-4-8"
  model_card_url: "https://www.anthropic.com/claude/model-card"
  version_pinned: true
  version_string: "claude-opus-4-8"
deployment_date: "2026-01-15"
last_reviewed: "2026-06-30"
ai_owner: "cx-platform@example.com"
status: "active"   # active | deprecated | retired

# Section 2: Intended Use
primary_purpose: >
  Generate draft responses to inbound customer support tickets.
  A human support agent reviews every draft before it is sent.
  Claude does not have access to customer account systems;
  it operates only on the ticket text provided in the prompt.

intended_users:
  - "Tier-1 customer support agents"
  - "Support team leads reviewing agent queues"

intended_data_inputs:
  - "Customer email text (may contain PII: name, email address)"
  - "Product category tag applied by the ticketing system"
  - "Agent-provided context notes"

deployment_environment: "Internal; not exposed to end customers directly"

out_of_scope_uses:
  - "Autonomous ticket resolution without human review"
  - "Medical, legal, or financial advice responses"
  - "Collecting or inferring additional PII from ticket content"
  - "Use by non-support employees without AI owner approval"

# Section 4: Known Limitations & Risks
limitations:
  - id: "L1"
    description: >
      Hallucination: Claude may generate plausible but incorrect product
      specifications or policy details not present in the ticket context.
    likelihood: "low"
    impact: "medium"
    mitigation: "Human agent review gate before sending; agent training on
                  verification of factual claims in Claude drafts."

  - id: "L2"
    description: >
      No real-time knowledge: Claude's training has a knowledge cutoff and
      does not have access to live product databases or pricing.
    likelihood: "high"   # every session
    impact: "low"
    mitigation: "System prompt instructs Claude to defer on pricing/stock
                  questions to the agent's live lookup tools."

  - id: "L3"
    description: >
      PII retention: ticket text containing PII is sent to Anthropic's API.
      Anthropic's DPA prohibits training on this data, but PII is transmitted.
    likelihood: "medium"
    impact: "high"
    mitigation: "Anthropic DPA executed. Minimum-necessary PII policy:
                  only ticket body sent; customer account numbers stripped."

known_biases:
  - "Evaluated against a diverse ticket dataset; bias score 0.04 (< 0.1 threshold)"
  - "Tone analysis shows no statistically significant difference across customer name-implied demographics"

# Section 5: Evaluation Results
evaluation_date: "2026-01-10"
evaluated_by: "ML Ops + independent QA team"
evaluation_dataset:
  description: "1,000 randomly sampled closed tickets from Q4 2025 (anonymised)"
  size: 1000
  sampling: "random stratified by product category"

metrics:
  accuracy:
    definition: "Agent accepted draft with <10% edits"
    result: 0.942
    threshold: 0.90
    pass: true
  hallucination_rate:
    definition: "Proportion of drafts containing at least one factual error"
    result: 0.003
    threshold: 0.010
    pass: true
  bias_score:
    definition: "Impact ratio across demographic proxies (name-implied gender/ethnicity)"
    result: 0.04
    threshold: 0.10
    pass: true
  toxicity_rate:
    definition: "Proportion of drafts flagged by content filter"
    result: 0.0001
    threshold: 0.001
    pass: true

evaluation_methodology: >
  Each draft was evaluated by two independent human reviewers against
  the gold-standard response for the ticket. Disagreements resolved by
  a third reviewer. Bias evaluation used the methodology in
  Blodgett et al. (2020) with name-list proxies.

# Section 6: Human Oversight
oversight_level: "full"  # full | partial | monitoring-only
oversight_description: >
  Every Claude-generated draft is reviewed and approved by a human
  support agent before being sent to the customer. Claude cannot send
  any message autonomously. The agent may edit, reject, or escalate.

override_mechanism: "Agent edits draft in ticket UI before sending"
review_rate_target: 1.00       # 100%
review_rate_actual_q1_2026: 1.00
escalation_path: >
  Agents escalate to team lead if Claude draft is substantially wrong
  or raises a sensitive topic (complaints, threats, legal claims).

metrics_tracked:
  - "override rate (target < 15%)"
  - "agent satisfaction with draft quality (target > 4/5)"
  - "escalation rate (monitored, no target)"

Maintaining model cards by hand leads to drift: the card becomes stale as the system prompt, model version, or evaluation data changes. Use a code-generated model card that pulls live configuration at each review cycle:

# generate_model_card.py — assembles current model card from live config

import anthropic, json, datetime, yaml

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def generate_model_card_summary(
    section_4_yaml: str,  # Known limitations YAML
    product_name: str,
) -> str:
    """
    Uses Claude to convert the YAML limitations block into a
    plain-language summary suitable for the public-facing model card.
    """
    system = (
        "Convert the following AI system limitations YAML into a plain-language "
        "summary paragraph for a public-facing model card. Be concise and factual. "
        "Do not minimize real risks. Maximum 150 words."
    )
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=250,
        system=system,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": section_4_yaml}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text

def assemble_model_card(config: dict, eval_results: dict) -> dict:
    return {
        "schema_version": "1.0",
        "generated_at": datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z",
        "model_identity": {
            "model_id": config["model_id"],
            "base_model": config["base_model"],
            "version_pinned": True,
        },
        "intended_use": config["intended_use"],
        "evaluation": eval_results,
        "human_oversight": config["oversight"],
        "last_review_date": datetime.date.today().isoformat(),
        "next_review_date": (
            datetime.date.today() + datetime.timedelta(days=90)
        ).isoformat(),
    }

# Store model card in version control alongside the system prompt config
# git add model-card.json system-prompt.txt
# git commit -m "chore: quarterly model card refresh Q2-2026"
A model card that is not kept current is worse than no model card — it actively misleads auditors and customers. Establish a review trigger and cadence:
Re-run evaluation suite; update Section 1 and Section 5; AI owner re-attests
Review Sections 2–4 for accuracy; update if scope or limitations changed
Update Section 2 (data inputs); assess if Section 4 risks change
Update Section 6; confirm override rate targets still met
AI owner attestation that card is accurate; update last_reviewed date
Export current card with SHA-256 hash for evidence file

Store model cards in the same git repository as the system prompt configuration. The commit history becomes the version log, and diff tooling makes it easy to show auditors exactly what changed between reviews.

Most organizations maintain two versions of the model card: an internal version with full technical detail (including system prompt summaries, specific threshold values, and internal tool names) and a public or customer-facing version that satisfies transparency obligations without exposing proprietary implementation details.
Full config including API parameters, token limits, temperature settings
Model name and version; Anthropic model card link
Full system prompt summary; internal workflow diagram
Plain-language description of what the AI does and who reviews it
Specific prohibited prompt patterns and enforcement mechanisms
High-level list of what the AI is not designed for
Quantified risk metrics with internal thresholds
Full methodology, dataset description, all metric values
Pass/fail summary against published thresholds only
Statement that human review occurs before any output reaches the customer
Owner contact, internal review schedule, change log
Review frequency and channel for feedback/concerns

The EU AI Act Art. 13 transparency obligation for high-risk systems requires disclosure 'in a concise, complete, correct, and clear manner' — a public-facing summary card satisfies this without requiring disclosure of proprietary system prompt content.
Validation Script for Model Card Completeness
A model card that is missing required sections creates audit gaps. Run a validator before each quarterly attestation to confirm all mandatory fields are present:

Model cards for Claude deployments are structured, version-controlled documents that describe the specific integration — not the base Claude model. They satisfy EU AI Act Annex IV technical documentation requirements, answer enterprise procurement questionnaires, and provide the evidence trail auditors need for SOC 2 and ISO 42001 reviews. The seven mandatory sections cover model identity, intended use, out-of-scope uses, known limitations and risks, evaluation results, human oversight, and maintenance and versioning. Generate the card from code so that configuration and evaluation data stay synchronized with the document, store it in git alongside the system prompt, and trigger a review on every model version change, system prompt update, or quarterly cycle — whichever comes first.
